patches.find: north pole source, treated as a circular aperture of radius ~500 km, has area A<sub>pole</sub> ≈ 7.9 × 10<sup>5</sup> km²; the sub-terrestrial return area across the full disc is π × 20,015² ≈ 1.26 × 10<sup>9</sup> km², giving an area ratio of ~1,600:1.
patches.replace: north pole source, treated as a circular aperture of radius ~500 km (estimated from model geometry; the dome never specifies this value — but even doubling the aperture to 1,000 km gives area A<sub>pole</sub> ≈ 3.1 × 10<sup>6</sup> km², reducing the ratio to ~400:1 and requiring B<sub>south</sub> ≈ 156 nT versus the fitted 64,852 nT: a 415× discrepancy, still catastrophically self-contradictory), has area A<sub>pole</sub> ≈ 7.9 × 10<sup>5</sup> km²; the sub-terrestrial return area across the full disc is π × 20,015² ≈ 1.26 × 10<sup>9</sup> km², giving an area ratio of ~1,600:1.
patches.rationale: ISS-603: Pole aperture radius (500 km) stated without source. Added model geometry disclaimer and sensitivity analysis showing the self-contradiction holds even with 2× larger aperture (415× discrepancy still catastrophic).

patches.find: The V50.6 → V51.0 architectural change was not motivated by any failed prediction or new observation — it is asserted rather than derived from a detected flaw.
patches.replace: The V50.6 → V51.0 architectural change was not motivated by any failed prediction or new observation — it is asserted rather than derived from a detected flaw. More critically, the toroidal architecture introduced a self-contradiction that V50.6's single-pole model did not have: a monopole model has no return path to conserve, so flux conservation cannot be violated by construction. By adding the toroidal topology in V51.0, the dome created a new quantitative internal contradiction (the 1,660:1 flux mismatch) that did not exist in the simpler version. The 'refinement' made the model quantitatively worse on its own terms.
patches.rationale: ISS-605: Strengthened V50.6→V51.0 transition argument. Added that toroidal architecture introduced the flux conservation violation that single-pole V50.6 did not have — the refinement made the model worse on its own terms.

patches.find: Gaia DR3 parallaxes independently confirm Milky Way Cepheid distances to ~1%, providing a geometric anchor that makes the distance scale framework-independent.
patches.replace: Gaia DR3 parallaxes independently confirm Milky Way Cepheid distances to ~1%, providing a geometric anchor that makes the distance scale framework-independent. A third independent rung, the Tip of the Red Giant Branch (TRGB) method, uses the sharp luminosity cutoff at the onset of helium flash in low-mass stars — entirely different stellar physics from Cepheid pulsation — and yields H₀ = 69.8 ± 1.9 km/s/Mpc (Freedman et al. 2019, CCHP, ApJ 882, 34), converging with Cepheid and SBF results within measurement uncertainty. Three independent stellar physics rungs (Cepheids, TRGB, SBF) agree on megaparsec distance scales, none of which the dome model can accommodate.
patches.rationale: ISS-611: Added TRGB (Tip of the Red Giant Branch) as third independent distance method. Freedman et al. 2019 CCHP gives H₀=69.8±1.9 via different stellar physics, strengthening the convergence argument.

patches.find: The 0.9941 EW scale factor can be compared to the globe's flattening f = 1/298.257 to illustrate the epistemological difference: the globe's flattening is derived from Earth's rotation rate and mass distribution, independently confirmed by four methods (satellite orbit perturbations, gravimetry, VLBI, SLR) each giving consistent results. The dome's 0.9941 is the output of a least-squares minimization on six WGS84-derived route distances — no physical derivation, no independent confirmation, no prior prediction. The dome's own AI context page lists 'EW scale 0.9941 physical interpretation' as an open question, conceding the parameter has no theoretical basis. A genuine dome-derived prediction would compute 0.9941 from disc radius (20,015 km), firmament height (9,086 km), or aetheric wave speed before comparing to city distances; instead, 0.9941 was reverse-engineered from WGS84 data and then claimed as a prediction.The 0.9941 EW scale factor can be compared to the globe's flattening f = 1/298.257 to illustrate the epistemological difference: the globe's flattening is derived from Earth's rotation rate and mass distribution, independently confirmed by four methods (satellite orbit perturbations, gravimetry, VLBI, SLR) each giving consistent results. The dome's 0.9941 is the output of a least-squares minimization on six WGS84-derived route distances — no physical derivation, no independent confirmation, no prior prediction. The dome's own AI context page lists 'EW scale 0.9941 physical interpretation' as an open question, conceding the parameter has no theoretical basis. A genuine dome-derived prediction would compute 0.9941 from disc radius (20,015 km), firmament height (9,086 km), or aetheric wave speed before comparing to city distances; instead, 0.9941 was reverse-engineered from WGS84 data and then claimed as a prediction.Unified angular coordinate
patches.rationale: EXP-023 integration: WIN-058 claim updated from 0.9941 scale factor framing to accurate V13 angular coordinate identity (θ = −lonE).

patches.find: Scale factor (0.9941) curve-fitted to WGS84 distances presented as independent confirmation; any parameterised model reduces residuals on its own training data
patches.replace: The identity θ = −lonE follows from the definition of geographic longitude, not dome physics; fixing V12's broken solar-noon proxy is maintenance, not prediction. WGS84 dependency persists (OPEN-001), and accuracy (7.3%) is 700× worse than standard geodesy
patches.rationale: EXP-023 integration: WIN-058 finding updated to reflect V13 claim (θ = −lonE is definitional, not a prediction).

patches.find: Dome's unified angular coordinate system with scale factor 0.9941 reduces cross-equatorial distance errors within the dome's own V13 framework.
patches.replace: The dome's V13 coordinate system introduces θ = −lonE as a 'unified angular coordinate rule,' replacing V12's solar-noon-UTC proxy. The dome claims this unifies both hemispheres with zero additional parameters.
patches.rationale: EXP-023 integration: WIN-058 detail_claim updated to V13 unified angular coordinate framing.

patches.find: WIN-058 is listed on the dome's wins page with a one-line claim but no supporting equations, derivation, measurements, or physical mechanism — it is effectively undefined on the dome's own site. The dome provides no detailed description of what 'unified angular coordinate' means: no formula derivation, no geometric basis from dome parameters, and no specification of what physical quantity it represents. Before evaluating a claim, the claim must be defined; WIN-058 fails this threshold. Unlike WIN-057 (which at least describes the two-zone topology), WIN-058 provides no justifying content: no formula for the 0.9941 scale factor, no geometric derivation from disc radius or firmament height, and no specification of what physical quantity the 'unified angular coordinate' represents. WIN-058 is listed on the dome's wins page with a one-line claim but no supporting equations, derivation, measurements, or physical mechanism — it is effectively undefined. Unlike WIN-057 (which at least describes the two-zone topology), WIN-058 provides no justifying content: no formula for the 0.9941 scale factor, no geometric derivation from disc radius or firmament height, and no specification of what physical quantity the 'unified angular coordinate' represents. The unified angular coordinate with scale factor 0.9941 was not derived a priori from dome geometry — it was fitted post-hoc to match WGS84 geodetic distances on six known routes. Any free parameter fitted to known data will reduce residuals; the question is whether 0.9941 was predicted before measuring. The dome's own AI context lists 'EW scale 0.9941 physical interpretation' as an open question — conceding the parameter has no theoretical basis. No prospective prediction states: 'Dome geometry predicts EW angular scale = 0.9941'; instead, the value was selected to minimize residuals when comparing dome coordinates to WGS84 distances. This is retrodiction (fitting known data), not prediction (forecasting new data). WGS84's flattening parameter (1/298.257) is derived from satellite orbit perturbations and independently confirmed by GPS, VLBI, and SLR to sub-millimeter precision — it is a measured physical quantity with a derivation from rotational physics. The dome's 0.9941 has no independent confirmation, no derivation from dome physics, and no prospective test. Furthermore, the V13 coordinate system still requires WGS84 distances as input (see the model's own OPEN-001 and OPEN-003 acknowledgments), making it dependent on globe geodesy rather than independent of it. Why 0.9941 specifically? The dome provides no derivation from disc radius (20,015 km), firmament height (9,086 km), aetheric wave speed, or any other dome parameter — only the fitted result. The value minimizes residuals on six WGS84-derived routes, making it a least-squares output, not a theoretical prediction. A genuine dome-derived prediction would compute 0.9941 from the disc's geometry before comparing to city distances; instead, 0.9941 was reverse-engineered from WGS84 data. For comparison, the globe's flattening f = 1/298.257 follows from Earth's rotation rate and mass distribution and is confirmed by four independent methods (satellite orbits, gravimetry, VLBI, SLR). The dome's 0.9941 has zero independent confirmations and remains an 'open question' on the dome's own AI context page. Standard geodesy achieves sub-0.01% distance accuracy worldwide using the WGS84 ellipsoid with two global parameters derived from rotational physics (Torge & Müller, 2012). The dome's unified angular coordinate achieves ~5–7% accuracy with multiple fitted parameters — more parameters producing less accuracy. The dome's 6.2% RMSE is 600× worse than Vincenty geodesy even after fitting a free parameter to known routes, and the dome's coordinate system still cannot handle equatorial routes at all (the dome's own OPEN-015 concedes near-equatorial cities 'fall beyond the range' of the model).
patches.replace:
WIN-058 claims that the angular coordinate in the dome frame is exactly θ = −lonE (negation of geographic east longitude), unifying Northern and Southern Hemisphere coordinate derivation. The dome presents this as a discovery: the V12 formula used solar noon UTC as a proxy for θ, producing catastrophic errors in the Southern Hemisphere (Sydney: +32.5° instead of the correct −151.2°, a 183° error). The V13 fix recognizes that solar noon UTC ≈ 12 − lonE/15h, so θ = (noonUTC − 12) × 15° = −lonE. The dome calls this a 'confirmed prediction.'

Kernel of truth: The dome correctly identifies a real bug in its own V12 coordinate system. The solar-noon-UTC proxy was mathematically equivalent to −lonE for Northern Hemisphere cities (where noon falls near 12:00 UTC) but broke catastrophically for Southern Hemisphere cities whose solar noon times produce angles in the wrong quadrant. Recognizing and fixing this is genuine model improvement — the V13 formula eliminates 180°+ errors in Southern Hemisphere cities.

Why this is not a prediction: The identity θ = −lonE is not derived from dome geometry. It is derived from the definition of longitude: geographic east longitude measures the angle east of Greenwich, and the dome's angular coordinate measures the angle west of Greenwich (since the dome's θ increases clockwise in the AE projection). The relationship θ = −lonE is a coordinate convention that follows from how longitude is defined — it is true on any map projection centered on the North Pole, not specifically on a flat disc. No dome-specific physics (disc radius, firmament height, aetheric wave speed) enters the derivation. The dome did not predict this relationship; it adopted the existing geographic convention it was already implicitly using.

The bug it fixes was self-inflicted: The 183° Sydney error existed only because V12 used solar noon UTC as an indirect proxy instead of longitude directly. Geographic east longitude has been available since the 18th century (following the adoption of the Greenwich meridian). The dome's V12 system introduced an unnecessary indirection (solar noon → θ) that garbled Southern Hemisphere angles. V13's 'discovery' that θ = −lonE is equivalent to recognizing that the proxy was unnecessary — the direct quantity was available all along. Correcting a self-inflicted bug is maintenance, not prediction.

WGS84 dependency persists: The θ = −lonE formula takes geographic east longitude as input. Geographic east longitude is defined by the WGS84 geodetic datum (or its predecessors). The dome's own OPEN-001 concedes: 'Dome-native coordinates without WGS84 — V12 uses longitude × 0.9941 as proxy.' V13 replaces the 0.9941 scaling with a direct longitude negation, but the WGS84 dependency remains — the dome still needs globe-derived coordinates as input. The dome's AI context page (V50.10) still lists 'EW scale 0.9941 physical interpretation' under OPEN-003, and the distance computation code (distance_analysis.py) still applies the 0.9941 factor: delta_t = np.radians(t2 - t1) * 0.9941. The V13 angular identity and the V12 scaling factor coexist in the codebase without reconciliation.

Performance context: V13's θ = −lonE achieves 7.3% mean error for Northern Hemisphere same-hemisphere routes — a regression from V12's 5.2%. Cross-equatorial RMSE improved from 25–78% to 6.2%, but this improvement comes from the two-zone topology (WIN-057's rSH = 2 × 14,105 − rNH), not from the angular coordinate fix alone. Standard geodesy (Vincenty inverse formula on the WGS84 ellipsoid) achieves sub-0.01% accuracy globally with two parameters derived from rotational physics. The dome's coordinate system, even after the V13 fix, is 600–700× less accurate than the system it takes its inputs from. Near-equatorial cities remain excluded entirely: the dome's own OPEN-015 concedes Singapore (r = 23,556 km) 'falls beyond the equatorial ring' at req = 14,105 km.
patches.rationale: EXP-023 integration: Full WIN-058 detail_evidence rewrite based on dome's actual V13 claim (θ = −lonE). Previous text attacked V12 strawman (0.9941 scale factor). New text: kernel of truth, why it's not a prediction, WGS84 dependency, performance context, code search findings.

patches.find: The 0.9941 scale factor is a post-hoc fit to WGS84 distances, not a prediction derived from dome geometry. Any model with a free parameter can reduce residuals on known data; this is curve-fitting, not prediction.
patches.replace: The identity θ = −lon<sub>E</sub> follows from the definition of geographic longitude, not from dome physics. Fixing a self-inflicted coordinate bug (the solar-noon-UTC proxy) is model maintenance, not a confirmed prediction. The dome's coordinate system still requires WGS84 longitude as input (OPEN-001 unresolved) and achieves 7.3% error where standard geodesy achieves sub-0.01%.
patches.rationale: EXP-023 integration: WIN-058 detail_verdict_text updated to explain why θ = −lonE is definitional, not a prediction. Dome still requires WGS84 input (OPEN-001), and accuracy is 600-700× worse than standard geodesy.

EXP-023 integration: Verdict change Not Demonstrated → Misleading. The dome presents a coordinate convention (θ = −lonE follows trivially from longitude definition) as a 'confirmed prediction.' Fixing a self-inflicted V12 bug is model maintenance framed as discovery. PENDING-HUMAN: cannot self-apply verdict changes.
